title: Turkish red lentil soup
subtitle: Mercimek çorbasi ,  bright, spiced, and very fast
excerpt: Red lentils dissolved into a deeply spiced vegetable broth, finished with a sizzle of butter and dried mint. The classic Turkish starter soup, ready in 30 minutes, with a tarka of paprika butter poured over each bowl at serving.
sourceNotes: Mercimek çorbasi (red lentil soup) is one of the most widely eaten soups in Turkey and the wider Middle East. It appears on the menus of virtually every Turkish restaurant and is a domestic staple. No single canonical source; method follows standard Turkish home cooking practice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the paprika butter finish. Keeps for up to three months. Make the butter fresh when serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles very easily. An excellent batch-cook soup.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Thickens on standing; thin with water when reheating.

glossaryTerms.definition: A finishing technique where spices are cooked briefly in hot fat and poured over a dish just before serving. In Turkish and South Asian cooking, this sizzling, spice-fragrant fat adds a final layer of flavour and colour that transforms the appearance of a simple soup.

content.text: Turkish red lentil soup is a soup of two textures: the smooth, blended base and the sizzling

of butter, paprika, and dried mint poured over each bowl at serving. The base is quick and requires nothing technical ,  lentils dissolve without soaking or blending when given 20 minutes. The paprika butter is made in a small pan while the soup warms and takes less than 2 minutes. Serve with lemon wedges and flatbread.

Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{carrot}} carrot and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{smoked-paprika}} of paprika and cook for 1 minute. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 30 seconds.
Add the {{lentils-red}} of rinsed red lentils and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes until the lentils have dissolved completely.
Blend until smooth. Taste and season with salt. If too thick, thin with a little hot water.
Make the paprika butter and serve

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small pan over a medium heat until it begins to foam. Add {{smoked-paprika}} of paprika and {{mint-dried}} of dried mint. Swirl the pan ,  the butter should sizzle and turn vivid red. Take off the heat immediately.
Ladle the soup into bowls. Pour a spoonful of hot paprika butter over each bowl. Serve with lemon wedges alongside.

items.symptom: Paprika butter is burnt and bitter
items.cause: The butter was left on the heat too long after the paprika was added.
items.fix: Have the bowls ready before starting the butter. Once paprika is in, the heat from the butter alone is enough. Take the pan off the heat after 15 seconds and pour immediately.

items.cause: Too much stock for the volume of lentils.
items.fix: Start with 900 ml of stock and add more after blending if needed. The soup should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.

items.cause: Very old lentils, or the simmer was too gentle.
items.fix: Red lentils should dissolve completely at a gentle simmer within 20 minutes. If still grainy at 20 minutes, increase heat slightly and cook for another 10.

items.cause: Insufficient seasoning, or lemon not served alongside.
items.fix: The lemon wedge is not optional here. A squeeze of lemon over the bowl transforms the soup. Season generously with salt and taste again.

content.text: Mercimek çorbasi is served in every Turkish restaurant and on family tables year-round. It is the soup made when the kitchen is nearly empty, and the soup that begins a proper dinner. The spice blend varies by region. In the southeast it is spicier; in Istanbul it tends to be smoother and thinner.
